title: Numeric and Integer Types
description: Learn about numeric (double) and integer data types in R.

slides.title: Numeric Type (Double) 🔢
slides.content:
In R, the default type for numbers is **numeric**, which is equivalent to double-precision floating point. Examples: `3.14`, `-2.5`, `1e3` (scientific notation).

```r
x <- 3.14
class(x)  # returns "numeric"
is.numeric(x)  # TRUE
```

slides.title: Integer Type 🔢
slides.content:
Integers are whole numbers without a decimal. They are denoted by appending an `L` suffix. Without `L`, numbers like `5` are still numeric, but you can force integer with `as.integer()` or `5L`.

```r
y <- 5L
class(y)  # "integer"
is.integer(y)  # TRUE

z <- 5
class(z)  # "numeric", not integer
```

Integers take less memory and are used in indexing.

quizData.question: How do you explicitly create an integer with value 10?
quizData.explanation: Appending L creates an integer: 10L. Without L, 10 is numeric.

slides.title: Explore Numeric and Integer
slides.content: Run this code to see the difference:
slides.codeSnippet:
x <- 3.14
y <- 10L
print(class(x))
print(class(y))
print(is.numeric(y))  # Note: integers are also numeric in R!

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content: Integers are a subset of numeric in R. So `is.numeric()` returns TRUE for both integer and double. To specifically check for integer, use `is.integer()`.
